quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Crystalline solid"?

options.text: A solid with a regular, repeating 3D lattice structure; types include ionic (NaCl), molecular (ice), covalent network (diamond), and metallic (Cu)
A non-rechargeable battery; the cell reaction is irreversible or impractical to reverse (e.g., alkaline, zinc-carbon)
The point where the indicator changes color; ideally matches the equivalence point; slightly different in practice
The number of moles of compound that dissolve per liter of saturated solution; calculated from Ksp and stoichiometry

quiz_questions.explanation: "Crystalline solid" is defined as: A solid with a regular, repeating 3D lattice structure; types include ionic (NaCl), molecular (ice), covalent network (diamond), and metallic (Cu). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Amorphous solid"?

options.text: Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
A solid lacking long-range ordered structure; molecules are randomly arranged (e.g., glass, rubber, many plastics)
A tube filled with inert electrolyte (KNO₃, KCl) connecting two half-cells; allows ion migration to maintain electrical neutrality

A weak acid or base that changes color at a specific pH range; chosen so its color change range includes the equivalence point pH (e.g., phenolphthalein, pH 8.2–10, ideal for weak acid–strong base titrations; bromothymol blue, pH 6.0–7.6, for...

quiz_questions.explanation: "Amorphous solid" is defined as: A solid lacking long-range ordered structure; molecules are randomly arranged (e.g., glass, rubber, many plastics). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Surface tension"?

options.text: The ONLY disturbance that changes K; increasing T favors the endothermic direction; decreasing T favors the exothermic direction
Gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are elastic, average KE is proportional to absolute temperature (KE = ³⁄₂kT)
The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
The energy per unit area at a liquid's surface; caused by net inward pull on surface molecules from unbalanced IMFs

quiz_questions.explanation: "Surface tension" is defined as: The energy per unit area at a liquid's surface; caused by net inward pull on surface molecules from unbalanced IMFs. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Viscosity"?

options.text: The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W
Acid: electron pair acceptor; base: electron pair donor; the broadest definition, encompassing reactions without proton transfer
A liquid's resistance to flow; increases with stronger IMFs, larger molecules, and lower temperature
The minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and form products; determines the rate's temperature sensitivity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Viscosity" is defined as: A liquid's resistance to flow; increases with stronger IMFs, larger molecules, and lower temperature. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Unit cell"?

options.text: The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)
A balanced equation that includes the enthalpy change (ΔH); coefficients represent moles, and the physical state of each species must be specified
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria
A substance that increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower Ea; regenerated and not consumed

quiz_questions.explanation: "Unit cell" is defined as: The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A solid with a regular, repeating 3D lattice structure; types include ionic (NaCl), molecular (ice), covalent network (diamond), and metallic (Cu)"?

options.text: Spectator ion
Standard reduction potential (E°)
Ion-dipole force
Crystalline solid

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Crystalline solid".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A solid lacking long-range ordered structure; molecules are randomly arranged (e.g., glass, rubber, many plastics)"?

options.text: Solubility product (Ksp)
Factors affecting rate
Amorphous solid
End point

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Amorphous solid".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The energy per unit area at a liquid's surface; caused by net inward pull on surface molecules from unbalanced IMFs"?

options.text: Ka (acid dissociation constant)
Surface tension
Metallic bond
ΔH from bond energies

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Surface tension".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A liquid's resistance to flow; increases with stronger IMFs, larger molecules, and lower temperature"?

options.text: Heat of vaporization
Ion-dipole force
Decomposition reaction
Viscosity

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Viscosity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The smallest repeating unit of a crystal lattice; common types include simple cubic, body-centered cubic (BCC), and face-centered cubic (FCC)"?

options.text: Colligative properties
Unit cell
Buffer capacity
Stoichiometric conversion

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Unit cell".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Solids and Liquids is accurate?

options.text: Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.
Chemical Equilibrium occurs when a reversible reaction's forward and reverse rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products.
Temperature changes alter K: increasing T shifts endothermic reactions forward (K increases) and exothermic reactions in reverse (K decreases).
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solids and Liquids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Crystalline solids have ordered lattice structures (ionic, molecular, covalent network, metallic), while amorphous solids (glass) lack long-range order.
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.
Stoichiometric calculations convert between grams, moles, and particles using molar mass and mole ratios from balanced equations.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solids and Liquids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
Liquids display surface tension (minimizing surface area due to unbalanced IMFs), viscosity (resistance to flow), and capillary action.
The half-equivalence point of a weak acid titration is where pH = pKa, useful for buffer preparation.

Solutions and Concentration describes how solutes dissolve in solvents through solvation. 'Like dissolves like': polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes, nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solids and Liquids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Properties like melting point, hardness, and conductivity correlate with the type and strength of bonding.
pH and pOH provide logarithmic scales for expressing H⁺ and OH⁻ concentrations. pH = -log[H⁺], pOH = -log[OH⁻], and pH + pOH = 14.00 at 25°C.
Lewis structures show bonding and lone pairs, with formal charge minimization guiding the best structure.
Kc uses molar concentrations, Kp uses partial pressures for gases (Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solids and Liquids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Solids and Liquids?

options.text: For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.
Only a small fraction of collisions are effective.
Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, or the number of particles increases.
Solids and Liquids exhibit distinct properties based on their intermolecular forces and particle arrangements.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Solids and Liquids as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Diamond (covalent network solid) as the hardest natural material: each carbon forms 4 strong covalent bonds in a 3D tetrahedral network, with mp > 3500°C
Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment
Combustion of hydrocarbons in engines: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O releasing energy to power vehicles; incomplete combustion produces toxic CO
Petroleum refining using fractional distillation to separate crude oil into fractions: gases, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oils, and asphalt

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Enzyme inhibitors as drugs: statins lower cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase, effectively raising the Ea for cholesterol synthesis
Liquid crystal displays (LCDs) exploiting the properties of materials that flow like liquids but maintain ordered molecular arrangements like crystals
Quality control in fuel production: measuring heat of combustion per gram to verify fuel grade and energy content
Rocket fuel calculations: precise stoichiometry of liquid H₂ and O₂ (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O) ensures maximum thrust without wasting expensive fuel

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Solids and Liquids applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
Radiocarbon dating using the ¹⁴C isotope to determine the age of organic materials up to ~50,000 years old

Tempered safety glass as an amorphous solid that, when broken, shatters into small granular pieces rather than sharp shards due to balanced internal stresses from thermal treatment (distinct from laminated glass, which holds together via a...

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Solids and Liquids?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Quality control in pharmaceutical manufacturing: acid-base titrations determining the exact concentration of active ingredients in drug formulations
Blood buffering: CO₂ + H₂O ⇌ H₂CO₃ ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + H⁺ with specific K values maintaining blood pH in the narrow range of 7.35-7.45
Diamond (covalent network solid) as the hardest natural material: each carbon forms 4 strong covalent bonds in a 3D tetrahedral network, with mp > 3500°C

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Solids and Liquids in practice?

options.text: Liquid crystal displays (LCDs) exploiting the properties of materials that flow like liquids but maintain ordered molecular arrangements like crystals
Drug elimination from the body often following first-order kinetics: dosing intervals based on half-life to maintain therapeutic blood levels
Supercritical CO₂ used as a green solvent in decaffeination of coffee and dry cleaning, replacing toxic organic solvents
Soda carbonation: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq) equilibrium maintained under pressure; opening the bottle shifts equilibrium toward gas release (fizzing)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Solids and Liquids.
